y private equity or melts into nothing. No, it=E2=80=99s not as glamorous a
role for Lazarus as being arguably the most important person at the Olympic=
s, but it=E2=80=99s a C.E.O. gig, and he will likely make a boatload of mon=
ey. He also gets to run a public company, albeit one still controlled by Br=
ian Roberts, the Comcast C.E.O., who will maintain 33 percent voting power =
over the new entity. That will set Lazarus up for bigger jobs down the line=
=2E

Also, it=E2=80=99s a chance for Lazarus to exceed the low expectations for =
these declining assets, whether by taking on more networks from Warner Bros=
=2E Discovery or Paramount Global or A+=
E Networks or AMC, launching niche str=
eamers for MSNBC or E! or Syfy or Oxygen, or adding stuff like sports or li=
ve experiences, all to slow the melt. If I were Mark, my first call for adv=
ice would not be to cable-era kingpins like John Malone or Jeff Bewkes but =
to the private equity bosses at Alden Global Capital who are managing simil=
ar fade-outs at newspapers. That=E2=80=99s his job now.

For Langley, meanwhile, this is a formal anointment. With Shari Redstone fi=
nally selling her father=E2=80=99s jewels, Oprah Winfrey becoming less mogu=
l and more personality-for-hire, and Disney=E2=80=99s Dana Walden still ove=
rseeing only television and streaming, Donna is now the most powerful woman=
 in traditional Hollywood. (Bela Bajaria at Netflix and Jen Salke at Amazon=
 still oversee larger content budgets=E2=80=A6 though neither has won a bes=
t picture Oscar. Langley has two.) And she=E2=80=99s paid her dues. Without=
 superheroes or a deep library of I.P., Langley and the team have developed=
 a fairly reliable pipeline with Jurassic, Fast, Illumination, DreamWorks A=
nimation, and Blumhouse. (We=E2=80=99ll forget about that whole =E2=80=9CDa=
rk Universe=E2=80=9D debacle.)

Perhaps more importantly, especially when compared to the remake/sequel mac=
hine at Disney, Langley has a stable of event filmmakers in Chris Nolan, St=
even Spielberg,
Jordan Peele, and potentially the Daniels, who are all making new, original=
 movies for the studio. And the post-Covid release strategy, prioritizing a=
 short theatrical window followed by a quick move to premium video-on-deman=
d, then S.V.O.D. and the rest of the waterfall, seems to have struck a sust=
ainable balance between the demands of commerce and the demands of creative=
s. Universal makes and releases more movies than any other studio, and many=
 of them miss (The Fall Guy=E2=80=A6 Renfield=E2=80=A6 Cats!?!). But the ra=
tio is pretty good and the formula is working, at least for now.

So credit to the Comcast guys for recognizing the obvious. One thing Philad=
elphia has gotten right from the beginning of their Hollywood adventure is =
acknowledging the fact that they=E2=80=99re outsiders to the culture. For y=
ears, they kept Ron Meyer in an undefined role at Universal that basically =
amounted to =E2=80=9Cproblem solver and industry
whisperer,=E2=80=9D until his personal behavior finally caught up with him.=
 Bonnie Hammer, who basically built the cable networks business they=E2=80=
=99re now spinning off, is still employed in a senior role in the TV group,=
 though I dare you to ask anyone what she actually does. She=E2=80=99s an a=
mbassador type, and that=E2=80=99s enough for Roberts and Cavanagh.

The Roberts Flex

Now, nobody wants these growth-killing cable networks. Bob Iger at Disney a=
nd David Zaslav at Warner Discovery have both talked about possibly selling=
 or spinning theirs off, as well. But the fact that Roberts can actually do=
 this is a huge flex for Comcast. Think about it: He=E2=80=99s siphoning al=
l that revenue thrown off by the cable networks business=E2=80=94about $7.5=
 billion this year, per a new Guggenheim Securities estimate, via $5.5 bill=
ion from distribution and $2 billion from ads=E2=80=94and his stock price a=
ctually went up. That=E2=80=99s because the rest of Kabletown generated abo=
ut $116 billion in revenue in the past four quarters, per CNBC ( https://ww=
w.cnbc.com/2024/11/20/comcast-cable-networks-spinoff-emphasizes-need-for-ch=
ange-in-media.html?utm_campaign=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F=
21%2F24%29&utm_content=3DWhat+I%27m+Hearing+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2811%2F18%2F24%2=
9&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da4=
2abd12a ). If Warner Bros. Discovery or Paramount
Global made a similar move, their share price would probably crash. They do=
n=E2=80=99t have the broadband or theme parks or channel distribution busin=
esses that Comcast can use to offset the loss of that cable revenue.

Disney has other businesses besides television networks, of course, but a s=
imilar separation would be a logistical nightmare. The Hulu streamer, for i=
nstance, is powered by content from FX, a cable network. Same with the ESPN=
 stand-alone streamer set to launch next year. Imagine if that service was =
part of a different company than the ESPN linear channel? They could figure=
 something out, of course=E2=80=94similar to how Lazarus may now populate h=
is networks with news and sports content currently controlled by NBCUnivers=
al. But it would be tough,
and probably why Iger floated then decided against a spin of his own.

But Roberts can do this, so why not? Cable networks are over as a growth bu=
siness, and they have dragged the stock down for a while. Plus, he=E2=80=99=
s never loved MSNBC and the political problems it causes for the rest of th=
e Comcast empire he does care about. Maybe the news networks, with their de=
dicated yet aging audiences, can serve as =E2=80=9Clocomotives,=E2=80=9D in=
 Comcast-speak, to drive the other network railcars and maintain carriage f=
ees without NBC (and its NFL deal) as that lead engine. With 60-something m=
illion subscribers to these networks and a clean balance sheet, who knows, =
maybe Lazarus turns the ice floe into a glacier of its own.

That=E2=80=99s the real challenge. Langley, not to mention Roberts and Cava=
nagh, still have their own secular hurdles in movies and broadcast TV, and =
Peacock is still not profitable. All of these spinoff machinations might ju=
st be foreplay to a
coming merger or similar transaction. But mostly Langley just needs to make=
 great content, and now she=E2=80=99ll have freer rein to do that.

See you Monday,
Matt

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Donna has been aski=
ng for this role for a while now, and her elevation finally settles the low=
-key turf war with <b>Mark Lazarus</b>, the longtime NBCU executive who con=
trolled sports, late night, and parts of NBC and Peacock. There wasn=E2=80=
=99t really a horse race for the top content job=E2=80=94at least not like =
five years ago when Lazarus jockeyed with <b>Jeff Shell</b>, who emerged wi=
th the C.E.O. title. But there <i>was</i> tension. Langley and Lazarus had =
their separate fiefdoms, yet he maintained Bravo, and they shared oversight=
 of Peacock. <b>Frances Berwick</b>, the chairman of NBCUniversal Entertain=
ment, reported to both Donna and Mark, often requiring her to secure two gr=
eenlights.<br/> <br/>That=E2=80=99s all clarified now. Langley will have ev=
erything, from if the dinosaurs go to space in the upcoming <i>Jurassic Wor=
ld</i> (or the one after that) to
whether <b>Pete Davidson</b> will get and then abruptly walk away from anot=
her Peacock sitcom (please, Donna, spare us), to managing the decline of la=
te night and a post-<b>Lorne</b> <i>SNL</i>, whenever that happens. She and=
 <b>Matt Strauss</b>, now chairman of NBCUniversal Media Group, will work o=
ut the organizational details soon. She=E2=80=99s been in New York this wee=
k starting this process. Lazarus, meanwhile, will run the spinoff cable net=
work company.  <br/> <br/>Is that a cool opportunity or a consolation prize=
?<b> Mike Cavanagh</b>, the acting NBCUniversal C.E.O., was careful in his =
memo announcing the spinoff to note that Lazarus and C.F.O. <b>Anand Kini</=
b> =E2=80=9Craised their hands=E2=80=9D to run CrapCo=E2=80=94sorry, <i>Spi=
nCo</i>=E2=80=94the ice floe of cable networks that, once broken off from t=
he NBCU glacier, will presumably journey through a sea of cost cuts and dim=
inished revenue until it eventually is bought by private equity or melts in=
to nothing. No, it=E2=80=99s not as glamorous a
role for Lazarus as being arguably the most important person at the Olympic=
s, but it=E2=80=99s a C.E.O. gig, and he will likely make a boatload of mon=
ey. He also gets to run a public company, albeit one still controlled by <b=
>Brian Roberts</b>, the Comcast C.E.O., who will maintain 33 percent voting=
 power over the new entity. That will set Lazarus up for bigger jobs down t=
he line.<br/> <br/>Also, it=E2=80=99s a chance for Lazarus to exceed the lo=
w expectations for these declining assets, whether by taking on more networ=
ks from Warner Bros. Discovery or Paramount Global or A+E Networks or AMC, =
launching niche streamers for MSNBC or E! or Syfy or Oxygen, or adding stuf=
f like sports or live experiences, all to slow the melt. If I were Mark, my=
 first call for advice would not be to cable-era kingpins like <b>John Malo=
ne</b> or <b>Jeff Bewkes</b> but to the private equity bosses at Alden Glob=
al Capital who are managing similar fade-outs at newspapers. That=E2=80=99s=
 his job now.      <br/>
<br/>For Langley, meanwhile, this is a formal anointment. With <b>Shari Red=
stone</b> finally selling her father=E2=80=99s jewels, <b>Oprah Winfrey</b>=
 becoming less mogul and more personality-for-hire, and Disney=E2=80=99s <b=
>Dana Walden</b> still overseeing only television and streaming, Donna is n=
ow the most powerful woman in traditional Hollywood. (<b>Bela Bajaria</b> a=
t Netflix and <b>Jen Salke </b>at Amazon still oversee larger content budge=
ts=E2=80=A6 though neither has won a best picture Oscar. Langley has two.) =
And she=E2=80=99s paid her dues. Without superheroes or a deep library of I=
=2EP., Langley and the team have develo=
ped a fairly reliable pipeline with <i=
>Jurassic</i>, <i>Fast</i>, Illumination, DreamWorks Animation, and Blumhou=
se. (We=E2=80=99ll forget about that whole =E2=80=9CDark Universe=E2=80=9D =
debacle.)<br/> <br/>Perhaps more importantly, especially when compared to t=
he remake/sequel machine at Disney, Langley has a stable of <i>event</i> fi=
lmmakers in <b>Chris Nolan</b>, <b>Steven Spielberg</b>,
<b>Jordan Peele</b>, and potentially the <b>Daniels</b>, who are all making=
 new, <i>original</i> movies for the studio. And the post-Covid release str=
ategy, prioritizing a short theatrical window followed by a quick move to p=
remium video-on-demand, then S.V.O.D. and the rest of the waterfall, seems =
to have struck a sustainable balance between the demands of commerce and th=
e demands of creatives. Universal makes and releases more movies than any o=
ther studio, and many of them miss (<i>The Fall Guy</i>=E2=80=A6 <i>Renfiel=
d</i>=E2=80=A6 <i>Cats!?!</i>). But the ratio is pretty good and the formul=
a is working, at least for now.   <br/> <br/>So credit to the Comcast guys =
for recognizing the obvious. One thing Philadelphia has gotten right from t=
he beginning of their Hollywood adventure is acknowledging the fact that th=
ey=E2=80=99re outsiders to the culture. For years, they kept <b>Ron Meyer</=
b> in an undefined role at Universal that basically amounted to =E2=80=9Cpr=
oblem solver and industry
whisperer,=E2=80=9D until his personal behavior finally caught up with him.=
 <b>Bonnie Hammer</b>, who basically built the cable networks business they=
=E2=80=99re now spinning off, is still employed in a senior role in the TV =
group, though I dare you to ask anyone what she actually does. She=E2=80=99=
s an ambassador type, and that=E2=80=99s enough for Roberts and Cavanagh.  =
